entry.title: Ebla Shared Bibliography on Zotero
entry.content:
The library is open and can be freely accessed: anyone can browse and download the referenecs there collected. However, only members can edit: anyone with a zotero account can become a member submitting a request on the group page. Please feel free to join if you want to contribute to the group. The group library is found here.

Norms

General

The following norms are adopted to allow citations in different style and to respect assyriological standards (i.e., particular formattings especially in titles).

  • Only references related to Eblaite studies are added.

  • Superscripts (<sup>...</sup>), subscripts (<sub>...</sub>), and italics (<i>...</i>) in titles are marked through HTML to support correct exportation in other formats. See for example that this

<sup>d</sup><i>ià-ra-mu</i> at Ebla

will produce this: dià-ra-mu at Ebla. Zotero does not allow formatting in titles, so this is the only available solution to guarantee a correct exportation in all other formats. Note that if automatic renaming in the zotero stand-alone on your computer is enabled, the HTML tags will be reflected in the file names.

  • The citation key field is not automatically enabled in the zotero standalone: Better BibTeX for Zotero needs to be installed (see also below sub Special Fields).

  • Besides all fields which are normally used in bibliographic entries note that:
    • journals are abbreviated only sub the secondary field Journal Abbr, while are written out in full sub Publication, to allow switching, if necessary, between abbreviated and non-abbreviated. If you want to export citations from zotero with or without journal abbreviations toggle (or untoggle) the corresponding option before exporting (‘Export item’ -> ‘Use journal abbreviations’).
    • Language is always specified, to avoid wrong capitalization of titles;1 languages are abbreviated as follows: en, de, it, fr, es
    • Short Title is always specified, to allow the Chicago short title style; on how to abbreviate titles in Chicago please see the Chicago Manual;2
    • the Extra field will contain an automatic citekey generated according to the following rule: author_abb_year.

  • For the list of admitted tags, see here.

  • Personal notes and tags added to the group’s references are public.

Other

Tagging and Collections

Tags in zotero can be divided into two groups: tags defined by the user (colored in blue, see the image below) and tags imported by default (colored in orange).3 Imported tags are general and might not be accurate: these tags are present if the reference has been imported via the ‘save to Zotero’ extensions for browser or via ‘add item by indentifie(s)’ option. User tags are instead manually added by the user in his/her personal library: these tags are used also in the group and are manually added by the editing members. To distinguish the group’s tags these are preceded by a !: this allows the visualization of group tags before other tags, and distinguishes the groups tags from the members’ personal tags.



Tags in zotero

For editing members: Note that adding an existing reference from your library to the group won’t reset attached notes and tags:4 personal tags does not necessarily need to be deleted, but group’s tags must be added according to those approved in the group’s tag list.

Collections are manually created by the members:5

Annotations

What can be done with a shared library?

  • references can be downloaded and opened with a different references manager software or imported in your personal zotero library;
  • formatted bibliographies can be exported
  • bibliographic resources can be searched
  • information can be shared via tags and notes

Usage

From in-browser

(#1): Selected references can be exported in several formats (Bib(La)TeX, RIS, Zotero RDF)

(#2): Selected references are formatted according to a citation style defined by the user and can be copy-and-pasted anywhere

(#3): Show/hide columns

Automatic citations via pandoc and pandoc-citeproc

  1. An exception is made when the title of the entry and that of the publication are written in two different languages, as in: Archi, Alfonso. 1993. “How a Pantheon Forms. The Cases of Hattian-Hittite Anatolia and Ebla of the 3rd Millennium B.C.” In Religionsgeschichtliche Beziehungen zwischen Kleinasien, Nordsyrien und dem Alten Testament, edited by B. Janowski, K. Koch, and Gernot Wilhelm, 129:1–18. OBO 129. Fribourg; Göttingen. In this case setting the language to en would produce wrong captalization in the German title of the proceedings volume. ↩

  2. See 14.33 in The Chicago Manual of Style. ↩

  3. For more information on tags see Zotero docs on tags. ↩

  4. This does not stand true for file attachments, which cannot be shared in a pulic group. ↩

  5. Automatic subgroups as advanced searches based on tags are not allowed in group libraries. ↩
entry.summary: The library is open and can be freely accessed: anyone can browse and download the referenecs there collected. However, only members can edit: anyone with a zotero account can become a member submitting a request on the group page. Please feel free to join if you want to contribute to the group. The group library is found here. Norms General The following norms are adopted to allow citations in different style and to respect assyriological standards (i.e., particular formattings especially in titles). Only references related to Eblaite studies are added. Superscripts (<sup>...</sup>), subscripts (<sub>...</sub>), and italics (<i>...</i>) in titles are marked through HTML to support correct exportation in other formats. See for example that this <sup>d</sup><i>ià-ra-mu</i> at Ebla will produce this: dià-ra-mu at Ebla. Zotero does not allow formatting in titles, so this is the only available solution to guarantee a correct exportation in all other formats. Note that if automatic renaming in the zotero stand-alone on your computer is enabled, the HTML tags will be reflected in the file names. The citation key field is not automatically enabled in the zotero standalone: Better BibTeX for Zotero needs to be installed (see also below sub Special Fields). Besides all fields which are normally used in bibliographic entries note that: journals are abbreviated only sub the secondary field Journal Abbr, while are written out in full sub Publication, to allow switching, if necessary, between abbreviated and non-abbreviated. If you want to export citations from zotero with or without journal abbreviations toggle (or untoggle) the corresponding option before exporting (‘Export item’ -> ‘Use journal abbreviations’). Language is always specified, to avoid wrong capitalization of titles;1 languages are abbreviated as follows: en, de, it, fr, es Short Title is always specified, to allow the Chicago short title style; on how to abbreviate titles in Chicago please see the Chicago Manual;2 the Extra field will contain an automatic citekey generated according to the following rule: author_abb_year. For the list of admitted tags, see here. Personal notes and tags added to the group’s references are public. Other Tagging and Collections Tags in zotero can be divided into two groups: tags defined by the user (colored in blue, see the image below) and tags imported by default (colored in orange).3 Imported tags are general and might not be accurate: these tags are present if the reference has been imported via the ‘save to Zotero’ extensions for browser or via ‘add item by indentifie(s)’ option. User tags are instead manually added by the user in his/her personal library: these tags are used also in the group and are manually added by the editing members. To distinguish the group’s tags these are preceded by a !: this allows the visualization of group tags before other tags, and distinguishes the groups tags from the members’ personal tags. Tags in zotero For editing members: Note that adding an existing reference from your library to the group won’t reset attached notes and tags:4 personal tags does not necessarily need to be deleted, but group’s tags must be added according to those approved in the group’s tag list. Collections are manually created by the members:5 Annotations What can be done with a shared library? references can be downloaded and opened with a different references manager software or imported in your personal zotero library; formatted bibliographies can be exported bibliographic resources can be searched information can be shared via tags and notes Usage From in-browser (#1): Selected references can be exported in several formats (Bib(La)TeX, RIS, Zotero RDF) (#2): Selected references are formatted according to a citation style defined by the user and can be copy-and-pasted anywhere (#3): Show/hide columns Automatic citations via pandoc and pandoc-citeproc An exception is made when the title of the entry and that of the publication are written in two different languages, as in: Archi, Alfonso. 1993. “How a Pantheon Forms. The Cases of Hattian-Hittite Anatolia and Ebla of the 3rd Millennium B.C.” In Religionsgeschichtliche Beziehungen zwischen Kleinasien, Nordsyrien und dem Alten Testament, edited by B. Janowski, K. Koch, and Gernot Wilhelm, 129:1–18. OBO 129. Fribourg; Göttingen. In this case setting the language to en would produce wrong captalization in the German title of the proceedings volume. ↩ See 14.33 in The Chicago Manual of Style. ↩ For more information on tags see Zotero docs on tags. ↩ This does not stand true for file attachments, which cannot be shared in a pulic group. ↩ Automatic subgroups as advanced searches based on tags are not allowed in group libraries. ↩

entry.title: Some tools for pandoc, bibliographies and academic writing

link.title: Some tools for pandoc, bibliographies and academic writing

entry.content:
The following is more or less a huge note to self on how I have been able to find and adapt to my needs several plugins/scripts. Hope other people might find it useful.

Markdown with pandoc-citeproc

To use citations for pandoc-citeproc in markdown citekeys are used [@citekey]

Creating automatically generated bibliographies with zotero and LaTeX

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------
%PREAMBLE 

\documentclass[a4paper]{article} %Defines the class of the document
\usepackage[T1]{fontenc} %Defines the font encoding used

%SPECIAL CHARACTERS
\usepackage{fontspec} %Used for special characters; requires XeLaTeX or LuaLaTeX

%SET PAGE MARGINS (OPTIONAL)
\usepackage{geometry}
 \geometry{
 left=20mm,
 right=20mm,
 bmargin=20mm,
 footskip=5mm,
 }
 
%SET HEADERS (OPTIONAL)
\usepackage{fancyhdr}
\pagestyle{fancy}
\fancyhf{}
\rhead{[\thepage]}
\lhead{Last update: \today}
\rfoot {}
 			
%LANGUAGE SUPPORT
\usepackage[german,french,american]{babel} %Supports typesetting in different languages

%BIBLIOGRAPHY
\usepackage[autostyle]{csquotes} %A package supporting different quotation styles

\usepackage[style=chicago-notes,backend=bibtex8,bibencoding=inputenc,url=false,eprint=false,isbn=false,doi=false,sorting=nyt,journalabbrev=true]{biblatex} %A package implementing bibliographic collections in the document

\linespread{1.1} %sets linespread

\usepackage{url}
\urlstyle{rm}
\appto\bibsetup{\sloppy}

\providecommand{\theendnote}[1]{#1}
\hyphenation{evans-ton clem-ens mc-hugh ho-ro-witz}
\setlength{\dimen\footins}{9.5in}
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\setlength{\parskip}{5pt}
\protected\def\onethird{{\scriptsize\raisebox{.7ex}{1}%
  \hspace{-0.1em}\raisebox{.2ex}{/}\hspace{-0.03em}3}}
\newcommand{\cmd}[1]{\texttt{\textbackslash #1}}

\usepackage{cmsdocs}
\usepackage{hyperref}

\bibliography{YOUR-BIB-FILE.bib}

%END OF PREAMBLE

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------

\begin{document}

\title{A title}
\author{Author}

\maketitle

\abstract{A short summary}

\nocite{*}

\printbibliography[notkeyword=nosample,title=References]

\end{document}

The fundamental command is \nocite{*} which prompts the generation of a list of all those references which have not been cited, i.e., all those contained in the .bib file. Download the full template here

DEVONthink 3

On this forum thread you can find an apple script for DEVONthink to convert markdown to PDF inside DT. I changed the command line options to use also pandoc-citeproc and convert all citations:

-- Construct your personal command line options here:
do shell script "export PATH=/Library/TeX/texbin:$PATH && /usr/local/bin/pandoc \"" & Path_to_MD & "\" -s -o \"" & theOutput & "\" --filter=/usr/local/bin/pandoc-citeproc --pdf-engine=/Library/TeX/texbin/xelatex --number-sections --bibliography=/PATH/TO/BIBLIOGRAPHY.bib --csl=/PATH/TO/CSL.csl"

To allow pandoc commands to work in scripts and applications you need to give the complete path (e.g., --pdf-engine=/Library/TeX/texbin/xelatex, not only --pdf-engine=xelatex). To find them, type which XX in Terminal (i.e., which xelatex) and the Terminal will give you the correct filepath. Note that you have to change the caps lock parts with your personal filepaths. The script is however not enough: you need to add a yaml to the markdown file indicating where the .bib and the .csl are located.

---
bibliography: PATH/TO/BIBLIOGRAPHY.bib
csl: PATH/TO/CSL.csl
---

Hazel

A rule for Hazel to convert markdown (or other) to PDF via pandoc-citeproc: here.

Application

After reading this post, I adapted the content of the command line and created a small application via Apple Automator to convert markdown to PDF via pandoc-citeproc. In Automator open a new file of the ‘Application’ kind: in the list on the left choose ‘Execute shell script’ and make sure to choose /bin/bash for the Shell and ‘as arguments’ for the input. The command is the following:

title=$(basename "$@" .md)
/usr/local/bin/pandoc -s --filter=/usr/local/bin/pandoc-citeproc --pdf-engine=/Library/TeX/texbin/xelatex --number-sections --bibliography=PATH/TO/BIBLIOGRAPHY.bib --csl=/PATH/TO/CSL.csl -o /PATH/TO/CUSTOMIZED/LOCATION/"$title".pdf "$@"
open /PATH/TO/CUSTOMIZED/LOCATION/"$title".pdf

As Raphael Kabo did in his application, a display notification comes in handy to know if the script worked or not.

Some pandoc / pandoc-citeproc command lines

To use pandoc on mac you need the Terminal app:1 everytime you need to convert a file you need to navigate first to the folder where the file is located. To do this just type cd /PATH/TO/FOLDER: the /PATH/TO/FOLDER will automatically appear if you drag and drop the folder in the Terminal window. After pressing enter, you can past or type a command and press enter again for the conversion.

  • A simple conversion from markdown to PDF: 
    pandoc FILENAME.md -o FILENAME.pdf

    To change input/output format simply change the extension. Files can be converted only to PDF, not from PDF.

  • From markdown to PDF via XeLaTeX: 
    pandoc -s FILENAME.md --pdf-engine=xelatex --bibliography=PATH/TO/BIBLIOGRAPHY.bib --csl=/PATH/TO/CSL. -o FILENAME.pdf

  • To number sections from markdown add --number-sections

  • To auto generate a ToC add --toc

  • To use a specific documents as reference for styling use --reference-doc=TEMPLATE-FILE.extension

  1. On how to install pandoc see here. ↩

entry.title: The City of Ebla - Supplement
entry.content:
Two reference collections automatically generated using Zotero and LaTeX, unsorted or ordered by author-date. These PDFs are meant for those who prefer a printed version of the additions to on-line bibliography on the Ebla Digital Archives Project. The online searchable edition is available on the EbDA website, and regularly updated (visit ebda.cnr.it).

For a list of the latest addition please see this page. For mistakes, errors, or omissions, please e-mail me at erica.scarpa@gmail.com.

Permalink to this page: erica-scarpa.github.io/ebla-supplement

Download links:

  • The City of Ebla - Supplement (sorted by author-date)
  • The City of Ebla - Supplement (unsorted)

Last update: 2020-02-29

EDIT #1 (2020-02-29)

ResearchGate feature: I uploaded the two documents also on ResearchGate. Note that ResearchGate has a feature that shows all references included in a document on the “References” panel in the document view. If the document has been uploaded to ResearchGate by the author(s), this panel provides direct access to it.
A similar option exists on academia.edu, but I have been increasingly annoyed by its click-baiting-like e-mails, and I am not going to update the page any longer, for several reasons see for example 1, 2, 3, and 4. Most of these issues affect ReasearcGate as well, but at least the latter is not spamming his users with tons of e-mails promoting paid contents subscriptions.

Links to the documents on ResearchGate: here (unsorted) and here (author-date)
entry.summary: Two reference collections automatically generated using Zotero and LaTeX, unsorted or ordered by author-date. These PDFs are meant for those who prefer a printed version of the additions to on-line bibliography on the Ebla Digital Archives Project. The online searchable edition is available on the EbDA website, and regularly updated (visit ebda.cnr.it). For a list of the latest addition please see this page. For mistakes, errors, or omissions, please e-mail me at erica.scarpa@gmail.com. Permalink to this page: erica-scarpa.github.io/ebla-supplement Download links: The City of Ebla - Supplement (sorted by author-date) The City of Ebla - Supplement (unsorted) Last update: 2020-02-29 EDIT #1 (2020-02-29) ResearchGate feature: I uploaded the two documents also on ResearchGate. Note that ResearchGate has a feature that shows all references included in a document on the “References” panel in the document view. If the document has been uploaded to ResearchGate by the author(s), this panel provides direct access to it. A similar option exists on academia.edu, but I have been increasingly annoyed by its click-baiting-like e-mails, and I am not going to update the page any longer, for several reasons see for example 1, 2, 3, and 4. Most of these issues affect ReasearcGate as well, but at least the latter is not spamming his users with tons of e-mails promoting paid contents subscriptions. Links to the documents on ResearchGate: here (unsorted) and here (author-date)
